summary: The article explores an experiment where teams practicing infrequent pair rotation (monthly or upon task completion) were challenged to rotate pairs daily for two weeks. The authors found that teams with infrequent rotations exhibited the same problems as solo coding—knowledge silos, difficult onboarding, and individuals becoming owners of specific code regions. Through daily retrospectives and structured facilitation, teams discovered that context sharing became easier with more frequent rotation, not harder. While no team chose to continue daily rotations, all dramatically increased their frequency, with one team moving from monthly to every three days.
key_points: Infrequent pair rotations cause long-lived pairs to become 'partners in crime' with context sharing becoming harder the longer pairs stay together
The perceived overhead of frequent rotation actually decreases over time as teams develop practices like documenting context in cards and maintaining to-do lists
Daily rotation makes knowledge silos impossible to maintain, forcing developers to work across unfamiliar areas with reduced risk since they only stay on a card for one or two days
Teams established core working hours and used timeboxing techniques like Pomodoro to address concerns about interruptions fragmenting pairing time
The experiment revealed that production issues could be handled by anyone on the team rather than depending on a single person with specialized knowledge
money_quotes: Long-lived pairs start to become 'partners in crime'
Context sharing gets harder the longer it takes for pair switching to happen
It was impossible to maintain these rules with the daily pair rotations of the experiment
No single individual owns any piece of work, and everyone in the team is responsible for the progress of the tasks as a whole
They found that when a problem came up in production, they didn't need to depend on just one person to look into and fix it
Information is flowing through the team
Knowledge silos are impossible to maintain
An incoming pair rotation brought new context that changed implementation direction and helped resolve a problem in the early stages
key_insight: The perceived cost of frequent pair rotation is actually an investment that pays dividends in knowledge distribution, team resilience, and reduced context-sharing overhead over time.
